bloody-mary.what: Bloody Mary is an American mirror-divination legend: chanting a name before a mirror is said to produce an apparition. Scholarship ties variants to 19th-century parlor and almanac traditions; Mass / playground oral tradition spread is documented from the mid-20th century onward.

when.earliest_appearance: 19th century — mirror fortune-telling and related ritual games in U.S. folklore sources; playground chain documented from circa 1960s in ethnographic accounts.
when.source: Folklore studies; North American almanac and schoolyard collections

bell-witch.what: The Bell Witch is a Tennessee haunting legend centered on the John Bell family (Adams, Robertson County). The standard published narrative derives largely from Martin V. Ingram's 1894 work, which claims basis in family papers; earlier oral and newspaper layers precede the book.

when.earliest_appearance: 1817 — reported disturbances in Bell household per later compilation; 1894 — first consolidated print narrative (Authenticated History).
when.source: Ingram (1894); prior regional oral tradition (not fully independently verified in primary contemporaneous record for all claims).

resurrection-mary.what: Resurrection Mary is a Chicago vanishing-hitchhiker legend tied to Resurrection Cemetery (Justice, IL). Multiple driver and bystander narratives accumulated from the 1930s onward; folklorist documentation treats it as an oral-tradition complex, not a single verified incident.

when.earliest_appearance: 1930s onward — layered sighting accounts near Archer Avenue / Resurrection Cemetery in published folklore record.
when.source: Chicago-area folklore collections; Crowe and successor researchers
